content.text: Healthcare agents apply the orchestrator pattern to one of the most constrained domains in software: clinical data is protected by HIPAA, clinical decisions carry patient-safety liability, and AI assistance to diagnosis or treatment qualifies as Software as a Medical Device (SaMD) under FDA guidance. A healthcare agent therefore cannot be built by simply pointing a general-purpose LLM at an EHR. It requires purpose-built architecture that enforces HIPAA boundaries around PHI, routes clinical reasoning tasks to specialized sub-agents, generates structured recommendations for physician review, and produces a complete audit trail for every inference.

attrs.alt: Healthcare agent architecture: EHR and lab data sources feed a HIPAA-aware orchestrator that dispatches to Clinical NLP, Differential Diagnosis, Drug Interaction, and Compliance sub-agents, with output routed to a physician-reviewed Clinical Decision Support layer

content.text: Healthcare is distinguished from other agent domains by three hard constraints that shape every architectural decision. First, data is Protected Health Information (PHI) — a legally defined category under HIPAA carrying civil and criminal penalties for unauthorized disclosure. Second, outputs that inform clinical decisions must be explainable: a physician cannot act on a black-box recommendation without understanding the evidence. Third, any software that provides diagnosis or treatment recommendations is subject to FDA oversight as a medical device. These constraints cannot be addressed retroactively; they must be designed in from the start.

A healthcare agent system is structured as a three-tier stack. The data layer retrieves structured clinical data from FHIR R4 endpoints — patient demographics, conditions, medications, lab observations, and clinical notes. The orchestration layer decomposes the clinical question into sub-tasks (NLP extraction, differential generation, drug safety check, compliance verification) and dispatches them to specialist sub-agents in parallel. The output layer synthesizes a structured clinical decision support (CDS) recommendation and presents it to the physician, who retains final decision authority. No data leaves the HIPAA boundary without de-identification, and every layer writes to an immutable audit log.
Healthcare agents address distinct clinical sub-problems, each requiring different data, tools, and safety properties:
Anchoring on early hypothesis; missed rare conditions
Alert fatigue from over-alerting on minor interactions
Patient profile against trial inclusion/exclusion criteria
Eligible trial list with criterion-by-criterion justification
False eligibility from misread exclusion criteria
Pre-filled PA form with supporting evidence citations
Incorrect policy version or missed criteria

FHIR (Fast Healthcare Interoperability Resources) R4 is the current standard for programmatic EHR access. A SMART on FHIR OAuth2 flow provides an access token scoped to specific resource types and a specific patient. The agent fetches the patient bundle — conditions, medications, observations, and clinical notes — then normalizes it into a structured context object before dispatch to sub-agents.

Free-text clinical notes contain diagnoses, symptoms, and medication changes that never appear in structured FHIR resources. The clinical NLP sub-agent extracts named medical entities and maps them to standard terminologies. The critical requirement beyond entity extraction is negation detection: "no chest pain" must not be coded as a chest pain finding. Specialized clinical NLP libraries such as medspaCy include negation context rules trained on clinical text, which outperform general-purpose dependency parsers for this task.

A practical pipeline combines medspaCy for entity recognition and negation with an LLM reranker that resolves ambiguous extractions and handles implicit temporal relations (e.g., "resolved 3 months ago"). The LLM is given the original text and the candidate entities as a structured prompt and returns a corrected, confidence-scored entity list.

Differential diagnosis generation must be structurally distinct from a single LLM completion. The agent maintains separation between (a) evidence gathering — assembling the patient's symptom timeline, labs, vitals, and history — (b) hypothesis generation — producing a broad list of candidate diagnoses without anchoring — and (c) evidence scoring — assigning each hypothesis a probability based on supporting and contradicting evidence in the patient record. Separating these steps reduces anchoring bias, which is the tendency of a single-pass LLM to over-weight the first plausible diagnosis it generates.

The output is a ranked list of diagnoses, each with a short evidence summary and a confidence band (high / moderate / low). The list is explicitly not a standalone diagnostic statement — it is input for physician reasoning, and the agent must communicate this framing in its output format.

A CDS system synthesizes the outputs of all sub-agents into a structured recommendation presented at the point of care. The key design requirement is actionability with context: the recommendation states what to consider, why (evidence), and what alternative actions exist. Vague summaries like "consider further workup" are insufficient. The CDS output format should match the workflow tool it surfaces in — a CDS Hooks response for EHR-integrated alerts, or a structured document for asynchronous review.

HIPAA defines 18 PHI identifiers that must be protected. In an agent context, PHI can appear in tool inputs, tool outputs, the LLM prompt, and the completion — all of which are PHI-bearing surfaces that must be controlled. The practical obligations for an agent system are:
Any LLM API provider that will receive PHI must have a signed BAA in place. AWS Bedrock, Azure OpenAI, and Google Vertex AI offer HIPAA-eligible services; consumer API endpoints generally do not.
Each sub-agent receives only the PHI fields it needs. A drug interaction checker does not receive the patient's name or address — only the medication list and relevant labs.
HIPAA requires a record of every access to PHI. Each tool call must be logged with timestamp, user ID, patient ID, resource type accessed, and model invocation ID.
Encryption in transit and at rest.
All PHI transmitted between agent components must use TLS 1.2+. Context objects stored in agent memory or checkpoints must be encrypted at rest.

Software that meets the FDA's definition of a medical device — software intended to diagnose, treat, cure, mitigate, or prevent disease — is regulated as Software as a Medical Device (SaMD). For AI-based agents, the risk class determines oversight requirements:
Predetermined Change Control Plan (PCCP) for model updates
Clinical validation data; post-market surveillance plan

The most practical strategy for reducing regulatory burden is to design the agent as a CDS tool — one that displays information to a clinician who makes the final decision — rather than as a device that acts autonomously. The FDA's 2019 guidance on clinical decision support software identifies this "physician as an essential intermediary" design as a key factor in determining whether the software falls under device regulation.

When an LLM API does not have a BAA — for example, a third-party embeddings service or a research model — PHI must be stripped before the call. The Microsoft Presidio library implements HIPAA Safe Harbor de-identification, detecting and replacing the 18 PHI identifier types. The de-identified text can be sent externally; the replacement map is stored server-side so the agent can re-identify mentions in the response if needed.

from presidio_analyzer import AnalyzerEngine
from presidio_anonymizer import AnonymizerEngine
from presidio_anonymizer.entities import OperatorConfig
import uuid

analyzer = AnalyzerEngine()
anonymizer = AnonymizerEngine()

# HIPAA Safe Harbor: the 18 PHI identifier types Presidio covers
HIPAA_ENTITIES = [
    "PERSON", "DATE_TIME", "LOCATION", "PHONE_NUMBER",
    "EMAIL_ADDRESS", "URL", "IP_ADDRESS", "US_SSN",
    "US_BANK_NUMBER", "MEDICAL_LICENSE", "NRP",
]

def deidentify_note(text: str) -> tuple[str, dict[str, str]]:
    """
    Replace PHI with deterministic placeholders.
    Returns (deidentified_text, replacement_map).
    The replacement_map can be used to re-identify if needed.
    """
    results = analyzer.analyze(
        text=text,
        entities=HIPAA_ENTITIES,
        language="en",
    )

    replacement_map: dict[str, str] = {}

    def make_placeholder(entity_type: str) -> str:
        uid = uuid.uuid4().hex[:8]
        placeholder = f"[{entity_type}_{uid}]"
        return placeholder

    operators = {
        entity: OperatorConfig("replace", {"new_value": make_placeholder(entity)})
        for entity in HIPAA_ENTITIES
    }

    # Track original values for potential re-identification
    for r in results:
        original = text[r.start:r.end]
        placeholder = make_placeholder(r.entity_type)
        replacement_map[placeholder] = original

    anonymized = anonymizer.anonymize(
        text=text,
        analyzer_results=results,
        operators=operators,
    )
    return anonymized.text, replacement_map


def reidentify_response(response: str, replacement_map: dict[str, str]) -> str:
    """Restore PHI in an agent response using the stored replacement map."""
    result = response
    for placeholder, original in replacement_map.items():
        result = result.replace(placeholder, original)
    return result


# Usage:
note = "Patient John Smith, DOB 1965-03-22, presents with chest pain."
deid_note, rmap = deidentify_note(note)
# deid_note: "Patient [PERSON_a3f9c1d2], DOB [DATE_TIME_b7e4a91c], presents with chest pain."
# Now safe to send to external API without BAA

Explainability in healthcare agents has two distinct audiences. For clinicians, an explanation is the evidence used — specific labs, specific conditions, specific note excerpts — presented alongside the recommendation. For regulators and auditors, an explanation is the complete inference trace: which model was called, with what inputs, at what time, and what was returned. These require different instrumentation.

Every tool call in the agent graph should emit an audit event to an append-only log. The log entry must include the model ID and version (not just a generic name), the patient ID, the access timestamp, the tool name, a hash of the input payload, and the output. Model version tracking is essential: FDA PCCP submissions must document which model version was used for which inference.

Human-in-the-loop in healthcare is not a UX decision — it is a legal and safety requirement. No agent should write back to the EHR, submit a prescription, or trigger a clinical action without an explicit physician confirmation event. The enforcement pattern in LangGraph is an interrupt node placed before any tool that performs a write operation. The graph pauses, surfaces the recommendation through the CDS UI, waits for a signed confirmation event (with user ID and timestamp), and only then proceeds.

The confirmation event itself is clinical evidence and must be stored in the audit log. If the physician overrides the agent's recommendation, the override reason should also be captured — this data is valuable for model improvement and is required by SaMD post-market surveillance plans.
The following shows a complete LangGraph implementation of a drug interaction alert agent with audit logging and a human approval interrupt before any EHR write.

from langgraph.graph import StateGraph, END
from langgraph.checkpoint.memory import MemorySaver
from typing import TypedDict, Annotated
from datetime import datetime
import operator
import hashlib
import json

# ── State ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
class AlertState(TypedDict):
    patient_id: str
    proposed_medication: dict          # {rxnorm_code, display, dose}
    active_medications: list[dict]     # from FHIR loader
    interactions: list[dict]           # populated by check_interactions
    recommendation: str                # populated by synthesize
    physician_approved: bool
    audit_log: Annotated[list[dict], operator.add]


# ── Audit helper ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
def emit_audit_event(
    node: str,
    patient_id: str,
    model_id: str,
    input_payload: dict,
    output_summary: str,
) -> dict:
    payload_hash = hashlib.sha256(
        json.dumps(input_payload, sort_keys=True).encode()
    ).hexdigest()[:16]
    return {
        "timestamp": datetime.utcnow().isoformat(),
        "node": node,
        "patient_id": patient_id,
        "model_id": model_id,
        "input_hash": payload_hash,
        "output_summary": output_summary,
    }


# ── Node: check drug interactions ───────────────────────────────────────────
from langchain_anthropic import ChatAnthropic
from pydantic import BaseModel

class Interaction(BaseModel):
    drug_a: str
    drug_b: str
    severity: str          # CRITICAL | MAJOR | MODERATE | MINOR
    mechanism: str
    clinical_effect: str

class InteractionReport(BaseModel):
    interactions: list[Interaction]

MODEL_ID = "claude-haiku-4-5-20251001"
check_llm = ChatAnthropic(model=MODEL_ID).with_structured_output(InteractionReport)

def check_interactions(state: AlertState) -> dict:
    prompt = (
        f"Check for drug interactions between '{state['proposed_medication']['display']}' "
        f"and the following active medications:\n"
        + "\n".join(f"- {m['display']}" for m in state["active_medications"])
        + "\nReturn interactions found, or an empty list if none."
    )
    report: InteractionReport = check_llm.invoke(prompt)

    audit = emit_audit_event(
        node="check_interactions",
        patient_id=state["patient_id"],
        model_id=MODEL_ID,
        input_payload={"proposed": state["proposed_medication"]["rxnorm_code"],
                       "active_count": len(state["active_medications"])},
        output_summary=f"{len(report.interactions)} interaction(s) found",
    )
    return {
        "interactions": [i.model_dump() for i in report.interactions],
        "audit_log": [audit],
    }


# ── Node: synthesize recommendation ─────────────────────────────────────────
synth_llm = ChatAnthropic(model=MODEL_ID)

def synthesize(state: AlertState) -> dict:
    if not state["interactions"]:
        rec = (
            f"No clinically significant interactions found between "
            f"{state['proposed_medication']['display']} and current medications. "
            "Safe to prescribe pending clinical judgment."
        )
    else:
        critical = [i for i in state["interactions"] if i["severity"] == "CRITICAL"]
        major = [i for i in state["interactions"] if i["severity"] == "MAJOR"]
        lines = []
        if critical:
            lines.append(f"CRITICAL ({len(critical)}): " +
                         "; ".join(f"{i['drug_a']} + {i['drug_b']}: {i['clinical_effect']}" for i in critical))
        if major:
            lines.append(f"MAJOR ({len(major)}): " +
                         "; ".join(f"{i['drug_a']} + {i['drug_b']}: {i['clinical_effect']}" for i in major))
        rec = "Drug interaction alert: " + " | ".join(lines)

    audit = emit_audit_event(
        node="synthesize",
        patient_id=state["patient_id"],
        model_id=MODEL_ID,
        input_payload={"interaction_count": len(state["interactions"])},
        output_summary=rec[:80],
    )
    return {"recommendation": rec, "audit_log": [audit]}


# ── Node: human approval interrupt ──────────────────────────────────────────
def await_physician_approval(state: AlertState) -> dict:
    # In production this node raises NodeInterrupt; the graph pauses.
    # The CDS UI displays state["recommendation"] and waits for:
    #   graph.invoke({"physician_approved": True}, config, resume=True)
    # For this example we model the structure only.
    return {}   # no-op — interrupt is configured on the edge


def route_after_review(state: AlertState) -> str:
    return "write_ehr" if state["physician_approved"] else "abort"


def write_ehr_node(state: AlertState) -> dict:
    # Only reached after physician_approved is True.
    audit = emit_audit_event(
        node="write_ehr",
        patient_id=state["patient_id"],
        model_id="n/a",
        input_payload={"action": "prescribe", "med": state["proposed_medication"]["rxnorm_code"]},
        output_summary="EHR write authorized by physician",
    )
    # ... actual FHIR MedicationRequest POST here ...
    return {"audit_log": [audit]}


def abort_node(state: AlertState) -> dict:
    return {"audit_log": [{"timestamp": datetime.utcnow().isoformat(), "node": "abort",
                           "patient_id": state["patient_id"], "reason": "physician_declined"}]}


# ── Graph wiring ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
builder = StateGraph(AlertState)
builder.add_node("check_interactions", check_interactions)
builder.add_node("synthesize", synthesize)
builder.add_node("await_physician_approval", await_physician_approval)
builder.add_node("write_ehr", write_ehr_node)
builder.add_node("abort", abort_node)

builder.set_entry_point("check_interactions")
builder.add_edge("check_interactions", "synthesize")
builder.add_edge("synthesize", "await_physician_approval")
builder.add_conditional_edges(
    "await_physician_approval",
    route_after_review,
    {"write_ehr": "write_ehr", "abort": "abort"},
)
builder.add_edge("write_ehr", END)
builder.add_edge("abort", END)

alert_app = builder.compile(checkpointer=MemorySaver(),
                             interrupt_before=["await_physician_approval"])
Healthcare agents fail in domain-specific ways that general-purpose guardrails do not address. Four failure modes recur in production systems and each requires a targeted countermeasure:

An NLP agent codes "denies shortness of breath" as a shortness-of-breath finding. Countermeasure: mandatory negation context checking before any SNOMED/ICD-10 assignment; validated with a negation test suite derived from annotated clinical notes.

Historical conditions or resolved medications contaminate the active clinical picture. Countermeasure: FHIR queries explicitly filter by clinical-status=active; the agent's context object distinguishes active vs. historical with explicit date fields.

An LLM invents a drug interaction not in standard databases. Countermeasure: drug interaction checks use a deterministic database lookup (OpenFDA, RxNorm, clinical pharmacology APIs) as ground truth; the LLM provides only explanatory text, not the interaction fact itself.

An overly sensitive interaction checker fires on every minor interaction, training clinicians to ignore all alerts. Countermeasure: severity filtering surfaces only CRITICAL and MAJOR interactions by default; MODERATE and MINOR are accessible on demand.

Clinical workflows impose strict latency budgets. A recommendation that takes 45 seconds to generate will not be used at the point of care. The agent architecture must be designed for the latency budget of the target workflow:
Deterministic DB lookup first; LLM explanation async
Pre-fetch FHIR on chart open; parallel NLP + hypothesis sub-agents
Async background job; streamed partial results
Batched offline job; results displayed at next session
Three architectural patterns are used in production healthcare AI systems, each with different tradeoffs:
Rules require maintenance; cannot handle novel presentations
Citable evidence; updatable without model retrain
Retrieval quality gates quality; hallucination still possible
Flexible; handles complex, open-ended clinical questions
Higher latency; more failure modes; regulatory complexity
Healthcare agents require HIPAA-aware architecture from the start: PHI scoping per sub-agent, BAA-covered LLM APIs, encryption in transit and at rest, and an immutable audit log for every PHI access.
FHIR R4 SMART on FHIR endpoints are the standard integration path; query filters (clinical-status, date) must explicitly exclude historical data from the active clinical context.
Clinical NLP sub-agents must perform negation detection — not just entity extraction — before assigning ICD-10 or SNOMED codes to findings in free-text notes.
Drug interaction checks should use deterministic pharmacology databases for the interaction fact and LLMs only for explanatory text, to avoid hallucinated contraindications.
SaMD regulatory risk class is reduced by designing the agent as a CDS tool with physician-in-the-loop rather than as an autonomous decision-making device.
Human-in-the-loop enforcement is implemented as a LangGraph interrupt node before any EHR write; physician confirmation events — including overrides — must be captured in the audit log.
